claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 17 and in approved education; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 17 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you find out if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

First, could you please tell me how many children you would like to claim Child Benefit for, and what are their names and ages?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Thanks for your help. I'd like to claim Child Benefit for my son Alex. He's 17 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information about Alex.

Since Alex is 17 years old (over 16), eligibility for Child Benefit depends on whether he is in approved education or training.

Could you tell me, is Alex currently in full-time education or training? For example, is he studying for A-levels, a BTEC, or enrolled in another approved course?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he is. He's studying for his A-levels at sixth form college.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's great, thank you. Studying A-levels at a sixth form college counts as approved education, so that's helpful for Alex's eligibility.

Now I have a few more questions:

Do you live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. That's good to know.

Now, could you tell me about your living arrangements with Alex? Does he live with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent, thank you for confirming that.

Just a couple more questions to complete the assessment:

1. Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that.

One more question: Does Alex work 24 hours or more per week, or does he receive any benefits in his own right (such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, he does work quite a bit alongside his studies - he has a part-time job at the supermarket that's actually pretty much full-time hours. But he doesn't receive any benefits in his own name.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. This is an important detail I need to clarify.

You mentioned Alex works "pretty much full-time hours" alongside his studies. Could you tell me approximately how many hours per week Alex works at his supermarket job? Specifically, is it 24 hours or more per week?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, it's more than 24 hours a week. He works quite a lot alongside his college work.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. This is important information for your eligibility.

Unfortunately, based on what you've told me, there is a rule that affects Alex's eligibility. Child Benefit stops if your child works 24 hours or more per week while in approved education or training.

Since Alex is working more than 24 hours per week at his supermarket job, even though he is still studying for his A-levels, this would mean you would not be eligible for Child Benefit for him under the current rules.

If Alex were to reduce his working hours to less than 24 hours per week while continuing his A-level studies, you would then likely become eligible to claim Child Benefit for him.

Let me provide you with the formal eligibility outcome:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on my assessment, here is the outcome of your Child Benefit eligibility:

## Eligibility Outcome for Alex

**Eligible:** No ❌

**Reasoning:** Alex is 17 years old and is in approved education (studying A-levels at sixth form college), which would normally allow continued Child Benefit eligibility for a child aged 16-19. You live in the UK, Alex lives with you, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for him. However, because Alex works more than 24 hours per week at his supermarket job, this disqualifies you from receiving Child Benefit for him. The rules state that Child Benefit stops if a child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week, even if they are still in approved education or training.

---

**What you could do:** If Alex's working hours were to reduce to below 24 hours per week while he continues his A-level studies, you may then become eligible to claim Child Benefit for him. You could consider this option if claiming Child Benefit would be beneficial for your family.

If you have any other questions, please feel free to ask.

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and is in approved education (studying A-levels at sixth form college), which would normally allow continued Child Benefit eligibility for a child aged 16-19. The claimant lives in the UK, Alex lives with them, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. However, Alex works more than 24 hours per week at a part-time job. According to the rules, Child Benefit stops if the child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week, even if they are still in approved education or training. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for their son Alex. Although Alex meets most eligibility criteria (aged 17, in approved education, living with the claimant in the UK, no other claimant), he works 24 hours or more per week, which disqualifies the claimant from receiving Child Benefit for him. If Alex reduced his working hours to below 24 per week while continuing his studies, the claimant could then become eligible.

The claimant lives in the UK, Alex lives with them, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. However, Alex works more than 24 hours per week at a part-time job. According to the rules, Child Benefit stops if the child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week, even if they are still in approved education or training. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

If Alex reduced his working hours to below 24 per week while continuing his studies, the claimant could then become eligible.
